nodes.body: Nadia bent down to retie her shoe, and when she looked up, the whole school group was gone. So was the guard who had led them out, and so, it seemed, was every light in the Ellery Museum except the dim safety bulbs glowing along the walls. She ran to the main doors and pushed. They did not move. In her coat pocket she found a note she barely remembered keeping, folded around a half-torn map of the museum, its paper soft and gray with age. Someone, long ago, had circled a wing that no longer existed and scrawled a line about a hidden collection the old founder had left behind. Nadia stood alone in the dark, empty rotunda. Three corridors opened off it, black and silent, each one waiting for her to choose.

choices.label: Slip into the Gallery of Gems.
Head for the Egyptian Wing.
Try the old Archive downstairs.

nodes.body: The Gallery of Gems glittered under low security lights, every ring and stone throwing off tiny sparks of color. Nadia's footsteps sounded huge in the quiet. At the center of the room, a big glass case sat crooked on its plinth, as if someone had bumped it and never noticed. She stepped closer, and that was when she heard it: a soft electronic beeping, faint but steady, coming from somewhere she could not quite place. It was not loud enough to be an alarm. Not yet. But it was enough to make her stand very still and listen before she decided what to do next.

choices.label: Study the big central gem case.
Follow the faint beeping toward a panel.

nodes.body: Up close, the crooked case told its own story. Someone had opened it in a hurry and shut it just as fast, leaving a smear across the dusty glass. A small vitrine inside sat tilted on its stand, and behind it, half hidden, a little printed card had fallen face down onto the shelf. Both looked disturbed, and disturbed recently, not the slow work of a hundred quiet years. Nadia thought of the note in her pocket and the founder's hidden collection. Whoever had been here before her had been looking for something too, and maybe, just maybe, they had left a trail without meaning to.

choices.label: Look under the tilted vitrine.
Read the little card that fell behind.

nodes.body: Nadia lifted the tilted vitrine an inch and slid her fingers underneath. Scratched into the wood beneath it was a small brass symbol, a circle crossed by two lines, rough as if someone had carved it in a hurry with a coin. She pulled out her half-torn map and held it up to the low light. The same symbol was there, faint but unmistakable, marking a spot behind the gallery. A narrow service door stood in the far wall, half hidden by a folded screen. The symbol pointed her straight toward it, and her legs were already moving before she finished deciding.

choices.label: Follow the symbol to the corridor.

nodes.body: Nadia turned the fallen card over. It was an old exhibit label, its ink faded to brown, written in a spidery hand she guessed belonged to the founder himself. Most of the words described a ring long since moved to another case, but one word near the bottom was underlined twice: corridor. She checked her half-torn map and found the same word marking a small door at the back of the gallery, tucked behind a folded screen. It was not much to go on, one underlined word and a fading memory of ink, but it was enough to pull her toward the narrow service door.

choices.label: Head for the marked corridor.

nodes.body: The narrow corridor smelled of dust and old floor polish, the kind used on floors nobody walked anymore. Nadia's footsteps echoed softly between walls lined with empty shelves. At the far end she found a small locked door, its keyhole cut in the same shape as the symbol on her map, a circle crossed by two lines. She checked her pockets. No key. She ran her fingers along the door frame and down the wall, and there, near the floor, a strip of skirting board sat loose, its edge lifted just enough to slide a finger underneath and pull.

choices.label: Pry up the loose skirting board.

nodes.body: Nadia followed the beeping past three cases of rings and one of loose, uncut stones. It led her to a small panel on the wall, its light blinking amber in slow, patient pulses, a motion sensor doing exactly what it was built to do. She was still staring at it when a beam of light swept across the far end of the gallery. A night guard's torch, moving slow and steady, sweeping from case to case. It was not pointed at her yet. But the beam was getting closer with every pass, and the gallery gave her almost nowhere to hide.

choices.label: Duck behind a plinth as the guard passes.
Freeze in the open and hope.

nodes.body: Nadia dropped behind the nearest plinth and made herself as small as she could, knees to her chest, breath held tight. The torch beam swept once across the spot where she had been standing and moved on without pausing. She counted the guard's footsteps as they faded, slow and unhurried, toward the far doors. When the gallery was quiet again, she looked up and noticed something she had missed before: a narrow service door in the back wall, standing open just a crack, as if someone had gone through it and forgotten to close it behind them.

choices.label: Slip into the service corridor.

nodes.body: The Egyptian Wing was all long shadows and painted eyes, rows of carved faces watching from every wall. In the center of the room stood a great sarcophagus, its lid propped open at an angle that did not look quite right for a display meant to stay shut. Nearby, a faded wall map showed the museum as it had looked a hundred years ago, with wings and hallways that no longer existed anywhere Nadia had ever walked. She stood between the two, the open lid and the old map, both of them daring her to look closer before she chose a direction.

choices.label: Examine the painted sarcophagus.
Trace the wall map of the old museum.

nodes.body: The sarcophagus lid was heavier than it looked, and it sat slightly off its base, as though someone had lifted it before and not quite managed to set it back straight. Nadia pressed both hands against the cool painted wood and felt it give, just a little. A few steps away, a glass case of amulets caught her eye instead, each piece nestled in its own dent of dark velvet. One dent sat empty, the velvet still holding the faint shape of whatever had rested there once, as if it had been borrowed long ago and simply never returned to its place.

choices.label: Peek under the loose lid.
Check the amulet display.

nodes.body: Nadia eased the heavy lid up another inch and slid her hand into the gap. Tucked in a nest of yellowed cotton wool, her fingers found something small and hard: a brass symbol, a circle crossed by two lines, the twin of the one scratched onto her half-torn map. She pulled it free and let the lid settle back down. A thin draught brushed her cheek, cool and steady, tugging at her hair from somewhere behind the wing. It smelled faintly of dust and open space, like a hallway that had not felt fresh air in a very long time.

choices.label: Follow the draught to the hall.

nodes.body: The little card beside the empty dent was labelled in the founder's own spidery hand, though the words themselves had worn away to almost nothing. What had not worn away was a thin arrow, scratched right into the glass of the case, so faint Nadia only saw it because the low light caught the edge of the scratch. It pointed away from the amulets, past the sarcophagus, toward a gap in the wall where a connecting hall opened into darkness. Someone had left that arrow on purpose, and it was clearly meant for whoever came looking after them.

choices.label: Take the arrow's direction to the hall.

nodes.body: The connecting hall was bare, just gray walls and a single small door at the far end, its keyhole cut in the shape of the symbol from her map. Beside the door sat a fire bucket full of sand, half a dozen rusted tools leaning against it like nobody had touched them in years. Nadia checked the keyhole for a key and found none. As she stepped closer, one floor tile rocked gently underfoot, looser than all the ones around it, as if it had been lifted and set back down more than once by someone in a hurry.

choices.label: Lift the rocking floor tile.

nodes.body: Nadia traced a finger across the old wall map, following a wing that simply did not exist on any floor plan she had ever seen at the museum. It ended somewhere behind the sarcophagus hall, in a space the building did not seem to have anymore. A display torch hung on a bracket nearby, unlit but clearly meant to be carried. Past a sagging velvet rope, a dim, roped-off gallery stretched away into shadow, the kind of space nobody official ever mentioned. It looked forgotten, exactly like the sort of place a founder might hide something worth hiding.

choices.label: Grab the display torch and press on.
Duck under the rope into the dark gallery.

nodes.body: Nadia lifted the display torch from its bracket and clicked it on, a thin, steady beam cutting through the dark. She followed the forgotten wing exactly as the old map drew it, past a turn she had never noticed before, into a narrow hall she was certain she had never walked in daylight either. The torch beam found a small door at the end, its keyhole cut in the shape of the symbol scratched onto her map. It looked ordinary enough, except that nothing about tonight had been ordinary, and this door felt like it mattered more than most.

choices.label: Approach the marked door.

nodes.body: The Archive smelled of old paper and older dust, the kind of smell that meant nobody had opened a window down here in years. A visitor ledger lay open on a wooden lectern, its pages soft and curling at the edges. Beyond the little pool of light from two reading lamps, tall shelves of the back stacks faded into a darkness so complete Nadia could not see where they ended. She stood between the two, the small honest ledger and the enormous unlit stacks, and felt the whole quiet room waiting to see which one she would choose first.

choices.label: Open the dusty visitor ledger.
Push into the unlit back stacks.

nodes.body: Nadia turned the ledger's pages backward through a century of visitor names, school trips and scholars and curious strangers, all of it ending abruptly on one blank page, as though whoever kept the record had simply stopped one night and never come back. Beside the lectern stood a card index, its little drawers labeled in fading ink, and below it, a low cabinet with one bottom drawer that stuck fast when she tugged it. Either one, she guessed, might hold whatever the founder had quietly filed away before the museum slowly, carelessly, forgot that he had ever existed at all.

choices.label: Check the card index.
Try the sticking bottom drawer.

nodes.body: Nadia flipped through the index card by card until one stopped her: it was out of order, tucked a full letter early, and stamped in the corner with the same brass symbol from her map, a circle crossed by two lines. The card's faded handwriting mentioned a locked cabinet, though not where exactly, only that it waited somewhere deep in the stacks, past the reach of the reading lamps. She slid the card back into place and turned toward the dark shelves, the symbol already burned into her memory.

choices.label: Follow the card into the stacks.

nodes.body: Nadia braced her feet and pulled hard, and the sticking drawer finally gave with a groan. Inside lay a folded plan of the stacks, its creases soft from age, one shelf marked with a heavy circle of pencil. She smoothed the paper against her knee and compared it to her half-torn map. The same symbol sat right beside that circled shelf, small and unmistakable. Whoever had drawn this plan had wanted someone, someday, to find that exact spot in the dark stacks, and tonight, it seemed, that someone was her.

choices.label: Head for the circled shelf.

nodes.body: Deep in the stacks, past shelf after shelf of forgotten titles, Nadia found the low cabinet exactly where it should be. Its keyhole was cut in the same shape as the symbol on her map, and just as before, no key waited anywhere in sight. She ran her hand along the shelf above it, a row of thick old dictionaries nobody had opened in years, and felt a shallow gap behind them, just wide enough to fit her fingers if she pushed her arm in sideways and did not think too hard about what might be waiting there.

choices.label: Reach into the gap behind the books.

nodes.body: The back stacks swallowed the light from the reading lamps almost completely, and Nadia stood at the edge of true darkness, shelves stretching away on either side like they had no end. Somewhere to her left, her eyes caught the faint outline of a shape hanging from a hook, round and heavy, exactly the size and shape of an old battery lantern. She could feel her way along the shelves instead, one hand trailing the spines of books she could not read, trusting the dark to lead her forward without ever really showing her the way.

choices.label: Feel along the shelf for a lantern.
Keep going blind into the dark.

nodes.body: Nadia reached up and her fingers closed around cold metal, a heavy old battery lantern hanging exactly where she had guessed. She clicked the switch, and a warm circle of light spread across the nearest shelves. Ahead, one shelf edge bore the same symbol scratched onto her map, a circle crossed by two lines, and behind it, half hidden in shadow, sat a low wooden cabinet she never would have noticed by feel alone. The lantern light had done in one look what her hands might have taken all night to find.

choices.label: Cross to the marked shelf.

nodes.body: Whichever way she had opened, the same thing waited in the dark gap beneath her fingers: a small brass key, wrapped snug in a scrap of oilcloth to keep off a century of dust. Nadia unwrapped it carefully. The key's head was stamped with the same symbol from her map, a circle crossed by two lines, and a short length of string tied a small folded note to its shaft, the paper covered in careful rows of letters that made no ordinary sense. She turned the key over in her palm. It looked old enough, and important enough, to open almost anything: the founder's study, a sealed cabinet, or the locked service stairs.

choices.label: Try the key on the founder's study door.
Try it on the sealed display cabinet.
Try it on the locked service stairs.

nodes.body: The key turned in the study door with a stiff, satisfying click, and Nadia pushed it open onto a room that smelled like sealed time. A roll-top desk sat against one wall, its curved cover half raised, and above it hung a stern painted portrait of the founder himself, dark eyes following her from beneath heavy painted brows. Dust lay thick on every surface, undisturbed for what looked like decades. Nothing in the room had moved in a very long time, which somehow made it feel more important, not less, that she had finally found her way inside.

choices.label: Search the roll-top desk.
Look behind the founder's portrait.

nodes.body: Nadia slid her fingers into the desk's rows of small pigeonholes, past old letters and dried-out pens, until she found a slim leather notebook tucked at the very back. Its first page was covered in the same careful rows of letters as the note tied to her key, half of some larger code waiting to be untangled. She closed the notebook gently and held it against her chest. Whatever the founder had wanted kept secret, he had split it in two, and she now held one half of it in her own two hands.

choices.label: Carry the notebook to the reading table.

nodes.body: Nadia eased the heavy portrait away from the wall on its hinge, and behind it sat a shallow safe, its door already standing open. Inside, the safe was otherwise empty, save for one object resting alone on the metal shelf: a small brass cipher wheel, its two rings of letters still turning smoothly under her thumb. It looked like exactly the sort of tool a coded note would need to give up its secret. She lifted it out carefully, feeling the portrait's painted eyes watch her the whole time, as if the founder approved.

choices.label: Take the cipher wheel to the table.

nodes.body: The sealed cabinet stood against the far wall, its glass lid protecting a stack of the founder's old travel journals, their leather covers cracked and soft. Nadia fitted the small brass key into the latch, and it turned easily enough, the mechanism giving a satisfying click. The lid itself was another matter. Decades of dust and dried varnish had sealed the hinge nearly solid, and when she pushed, it barely shifted. Whatever answers those journals held, they were not going to come easily, and she would have to decide how hard she was willing to try.

choices.label: Ease the cabinet open.
Force the stubborn latch.

nodes.body: Nadia worked the lid slowly, rocking it side to side instead of forcing it straight up, coaxing the old hinge loose a fraction at a time. It gave way at last without a sound, swinging open smooth and silent. Tucked among the travel journals lay a slim ledger, a faded ribbon marking one particular page. She opened it there and found rows of careful, coded lines, clearly meant to be read alongside something else. Whoever had hidden this had wanted it found gently, by someone patient enough to take their time.

choices.label: Lift out the bookmarked ledger.

nodes.body: The coded lines in the ledger matched the pattern of the note tied to her key almost exactly, the same careful rows of letters and numbers. Nadia closed the ledger and looked around the study. Near the window stood a small reading table, a green-shaded lamp waiting on it, unlit but ready, as if the room had been left set up for exactly this moment. It looked like the right place to spread everything out and finally start making sense of what she had found.

choices.label: Spread the ledger on the reading table.

nodes.body: Nadia leaned her weight against the stubborn latch, and the old glass answered with a long, low creak that made her freeze. It was a warning sound, the kind glass makes right before it decides whether to bend or break. She could feel the lid give a little under the pressure, stiff but not quite stuck for good. Ease off now, and try something gentler. Or give it one firm yank and hope the glass held. Either way, she had only a second or two left to decide before her arms gave out on their own.

choices.label: Steady it before the glass cracks.
Yank it hard.

nodes.body: Nadia let go of the pressure and took a slow breath instead. She lifted again, gently this time, easing the lid rather than forcing it, and the old hinge finally surrendered with a soft, grateful sigh. The bookmarked ledger slid free into her hands, falling open of its own accord to the same page of coded lines, uppermost and waiting. She carried it toward a small table by the window, settling into the chair there, ready at last to read the code all the way through.

choices.label: Settle in to read the code.

nodes.body: The key slid into the service stairs' lock and turned with a heavy clunk, and the door swung inward onto a narrow stairwell climbing up into shadow. Cool air drifted down from somewhere above. At the base of the stairs, tucked beside the bottom step, stood a small cupboard, its door hanging slightly open. Something pale showed through the gap, hard to make out in the dim light. Nadia glanced up the dark stairs, then down at the cupboard, weighing which direction was more likely to reward a little more curiosity.

choices.label: Climb toward the landing.
Check the cupboard at the base.

nodes.body: Nadia climbed the narrow stairs, one careful step at a time, until they opened onto a small landing she had not expected. A reading table stood there, its green lamp somehow still glowing after all these years, casting a warm circle of light. Beside the lamp sat a folded coded note and a small brass cipher wheel, laid out together as if someone, long ago, had meant for them to be found exactly like that, side by side, waiting for the right person to arrive.

choices.label: Sit at the reading table.

nodes.body: Nadia crouched and opened the small cupboard the rest of the way. Inside lay a rolled chart, its edges brittle, and beside it a pair of the founder's own spare spectacles, the lenses thick and slightly scratched. She unrolled the chart just enough to see rows of coded lines marching across the paper. It was clearly meant to be studied somewhere calm and well lit, not crouched in a stairwell, and she rolled it back up carefully to carry toward a proper table.

choices.label: Take the chart up to the reading table.

nodes.body: Whatever the night had given her, it seemed to belong on the small reading table under the green lamp, and Nadia set it down among the note's careful rows of letters, already smoothed flat and waiting. She pulled the chair close and looked at everything gathered there: the code from her pocket, and whatever tools or clues her search through the museum had turned up along the way. It felt, in that quiet room, like following exactly the same path the founder must have walked himself, years before she was born. She leaned into the lamplight and began to read the first line.

choices.label: Read the first line of the cipher.

nodes.body: Letter by letter, Nadia worked the code loose, crossing out false starts and trying again until the first line finally gave up its meaning: follow the stars. Three words, and beneath them, in smaller letters, three room names circled the museum's central rotunda like points on a compass: the Astronomy Hall, the Map Room, and the Clock Corridor. She read the line twice to be sure, then looked up from the page. Whichever door the founder had truly meant, there was only one way to find out. She folded the note back into her pocket and got to her feet, ready to choose.

choices.label: Head to the Astronomy Hall the cipher names.
Head to the Map Room.
Head down the Clock Corridor.

nodes.body: The Astronomy Hall glimmered under a vast painted dome, constellations picked out in flaking gold against deep blue plaster. A spiral staircase wound up along the curved wall, climbing toward the dome itself, slow and formal. Off to one side, half hidden behind a velvet curtain, a plain metal maintenance ladder offered a faster, less graceful way up. Nadia tipped her head back, taking in the painted stars, and felt the cipher's first line settle over her again. Follow the stars, it had said. Now she just had to decide how she meant to reach them.

choices.label: Climb toward the star dome.
Take the maintenance ladder.

nodes.body: Nadia climbed the spiral stairs until she stood right beneath the painted dome, close enough to see brushstrokes in the gold. One constellation stood out from the rest, its stars connected by a faint painted line that matched the shape from the cipher's clue exactly. Below it, tucked into the curved wall, a small marked door waited, easy to miss unless you knew to look. She pressed it open and found a passage sloping gently downward, clearly leading back toward the rotunda at the very heart of the museum.

choices.label: Take the marked door to the rotunda.

nodes.body: The maintenance ladder rose higher than Nadia expected, its rungs cold and slightly rusted under her palms. At the top, a narrow catwalk stretched across the hall, high above the painted floor. A steady walk along it led toward a small service door on the far side, solid and reachable if she kept her balance and her nerve. But above the catwalk, the dome's shadowed upper reaches climbed further still, dark and unexplored, and some small reckless part of her wanted to see exactly how far up this ladder was willing to take her.

choices.label: Edge across the catwalk.
Climb higher into the dark dome.

nodes.body: Nadia kept her eyes fixed straight ahead and her hands loose at her sides, walking the catwalk one careful step at a time instead of looking down at the long drop to the painted floor. The metal barely creaked beneath her sneakers. At the far end, the service door opened easily onto a narrow stair leading down, cool air rising to meet her. She followed it, feeling the museum's usual quiet settle back around her, and the stair let her out somewhere close to the rotunda at last.

choices.label: Descend toward the rotunda.

nodes.body: The Map Room was lined floor to ceiling with old charts, their colors faded to soft browns and blues. In the center stood a great floor globe, taller than Nadia herself, mounted on gleaming brass rails that let it turn with a touch. Nearby, a giant bound atlas lay open on a slanted wooden stand, its pages nearly as wide as she was tall. The cipher's line still echoed in her head: follow the stars. Somewhere between the globe's painted latitudes and the atlas's careful ink, she guessed, the room held its own kind of sky.

choices.label: Spin the great floor globe.
Open the giant atlas.

nodes.body: Nadia set her palms against the great globe and turned it slowly, watching the painted latitudes slide past until she reached the line the cipher had pointed her toward. The globe clicked, once, softly, like a key turning somewhere deep inside its brass frame. Along the base of the nearest wall, a low panel swung aside on a hinge she had not noticed before, opening onto a narrow passage. Cool air drifted out of it, and somewhere beyond, she could feel the pull of the rotunda waiting.

choices.label: Step through toward the rotunda.

nodes.body: Nadia turned the atlas's huge pages carefully until, near the back, one fell open on its own to a hand-drawn map that did not match the printed pages around it. It was the founder's own work, she was almost sure, added long after the atlas was bound. A route ran across it in faded red ink, winding past rooms she recognized and some she did not, all of it aimed toward the very center of the museum. She traced the line with one finger, memorizing every turn before she closed the heavy book.

choices.label: Follow the inked red route.

nodes.body: Nadia followed the inked red route exactly as it was drawn, down a back stair she would never have found on her own and through a cramped store room stacked with crates and folded canvas. The path bent twice, just as the map had shown, and came out precisely where the founder's ink had promised: a plain door standing at the edge of the central rotunda. She pressed her palm flat against the wood for a moment, half expecting the whole thing to have been a trick, before she pushed it open.

choices.label: Open the rotunda door.

nodes.body: The Clock Corridor ticked with the sound of a hundred old clocks, all slightly out of time with each other, so the air itself seemed to hum. Cases of them lined both walls, brass pendulums swinging behind glass. At one end, a small doorway led back into a gear room where the real machinery lived. At the other, a much larger clock loomed over a second doorway, its great pendulum swinging slow and heavy across the only path through. Nadia stood between the two, listening to a hundred seconds ticking past at once, and chose.

choices.label: Follow the ticking to the gear room.
Cross under the great pendulum.

nodes.body: The gear room smelled of oil and warm metal, wheels and springs turning quietly behind a glass panel. In the middle stood the master clock, its hands sitting just slightly off the hour the cipher had given her. Nadia reached in carefully and nudged the minute hand forward, then the hour hand, until both matched exactly. Somewhere below her feet, a mechanism clicked and released, and a narrow maintenance door swung open along the far wall, leading, she was fairly sure, straight back toward the rotunda.

choices.label: Go through the maintenance door.

nodes.body: The great pendulum swung in a slow, steady beat across the only doorway out of the corridor, close enough on each pass that Nadia could feel the air move against her face. There was no way around it, only through, timed carefully between swings. She watched it arc away from her, counting the beat under her breath, one and two and three. She could wait for the rhythm to feel certain and slip beneath it at just the right moment, or trust her legs and rush across while it still hung far to one side.

choices.label: Time your steps past the pendulum.
Rush across before it swings back.

nodes.body: Nadia counted the beat one more time, waited for the pendulum to swing fully away, and walked through steady and unhurried, right on the rhythm she had learned. It passed behind her with a soft whoosh of displaced air, missing her by a comfortable margin. On the other side, a plain passage sloped gently downward, the ticking of the clocks fading behind her with every step, until she could feel, more than see, that she was nearing the rotunda at last.

choices.label: Follow the passage to the rotunda.

nodes.body: Whichever passage had carried her there, all of them opened, in the end, onto the same room: the central rotunda, its glass dome arching high overhead and the night sky faintly visible through the grime. In the middle stood a great clockwork globe built from rings of brass, gears nested inside gears, silent and enormous. The cipher had promised that the founder's real collection waited somewhere in this very room, not hidden in some distant wing but right at the heart of everything. Nadia circled the globe once, taking in its pedestal and the darkened doorway marked as the night guard's records room, and began to plan.

choices.label: Align the clockwork globe to the cipher.
Search the pedestal for a hidden panel.
Slip into the night-guard's records room.

nodes.body: Nadia reached up and set her hands against the nearest ring of brass. It turned with barely a whisper, smoother than she expected for something so old. The cipher had given her an exact setting, a string of numbers she had already memorized on the stairs down, but the rings themselves were stiff in places and eager to spin right past the mark if she was not careful. She lined her thumb up with the first number, feeling the mechanism's quiet resistance, and understood that this was going to take patience more than strength.

choices.label: Set the rings exactly as decoded.
Spin the rings by feel.

nodes.body: One by one, Nadia set each ring to its decoded mark, checking and rechecking against the numbers from the cipher before she let go. When the last ring clicked into place, the whole globe seemed to sigh, gears meshing somewhere deep inside its brass frame. A section of the marble floor beneath the pedestal shuddered, then slid smoothly back, revealing a narrow stair spiraling down into darkness. Cool air rose to meet her, carrying the faint smell of old stone and something else she could not quite name.

choices.label: Descend the hidden stair.

nodes.body: Nadia decided to trust her hands instead of the numbers and spun the rings by feel, guessing at a pattern that felt right. It was wrong. The rings clunked into place with a sound that was almost a complaint, and the whole globe let out a low groan, as if the mechanism itself disapproved. Nothing opened. She stood back, breathing hard, and considered her options. She could start over, slow down, and decode the cipher's numbers properly this time, or she could lean her whole weight into the stubborn rings and simply force them where she wanted.

choices.label: Start over and decode it properly.
Force the rings into place.

nodes.body: Nadia reset every ring to its starting position and made herself slow down, reading the cipher's numbers twice before she touched anything. One at a time, she turned each ring to its true mark, checking her work as she went instead of trusting her memory. This time, when the last ring clicked home, the globe's gears meshed cleanly and the marble floor slid open just as it should have the first time, revealing the same narrow stair curling down into the dark below.

choices.label: Take the stair down.

nodes.body: The pedestal beneath the great globe was carved all over with small sliding tiles, each one etched with a tiny symbol worn smooth by years of curious fingers, or perhaps just one very patient founder. Running her hand along its base, Nadia found one seam that breathed a thin, cool draught against her palm, different from the rest of the stonework. It hinted at something hollow behind it, a hidden door as much as a puzzle. She could work the tiles into some kind of order, or follow the draught and see where it led instead.

choices.label: Solve the little sliding puzzle.
Follow the draught to the side door.

nodes.body: Nadia slid the tiles patiently, one at a time, matching each faint symbol until they formed the same shape scratched onto her half-torn map, a circle crossed by two lines. The last tile clicked home, and somewhere beneath the pedestal a hidden catch released with a soft, satisfying snap. The marble floor around the base shifted and drew back, opening onto a narrow stair that curled away into the dark, exactly the kind of hidden passage the whole night had been leading her toward.

choices.label: Go down the hidden stair.

nodes.body: Nadia followed the cool draught instead, tracing it around the pedestal to a low side door tucked into the curve of the wall, the kind of door no floor plan of the museum had ever shown her. It opened without much resistance, hinges stiff but willing. Beyond it lay a small, dusty room crowded with half-built clockwork figures and rows of glass cases, all of it forgotten and none of it, as far as she could tell, part of any tour the museum had ever given.

choices.label: Step into the hidden room.

nodes.body: This, Nadia realized slowly, was the founder's own workshop, sealed up and forgotten long before she was born. Long benches ran along every wall, crowded with unfinished automata frozen mid-gesture, tiny brass birds and clockwork animals labeled in his same spidery hand. It was not the hidden treasure the cipher had promised her, no gold, no vault of jewels. But standing among all those half-finished wonders, gathering dust in the dark for a hundred years, felt like its own kind of discovery, quieter than gold and somehow just as rare.

choices.label: Take it all in.

nodes.body: The night guard's records room hummed softly with a bank of old monitors, their screens flickering gray and grainy over empty hallways. A rounds log lay open on the desk, its pages filled with tidy handwriting and timestamps stretching back weeks. Beside it sat a heavy black telephone, its cord coiled and its receiver close enough to reach without moving. Nadia glanced between the log and the phone, the whole museum's quiet routine laid out in front of her, and wondered which one held more of what she needed tonight.

choices.label: Read the guard's rounds log.
Use the desk phone.

nodes.body: Nadia flipped the rounds log back past weeks of ordinary entries until she reached the very oldest page, written not by any guard but in the founder's own careful hand. It noted, plainly, that a stair opened beneath the globe at a certain hour, the same hour the cipher had given her. She checked the clock on the wall and bolted back toward the rotunda. She arrived just as the marble floor beneath the pedestal was already sliding open on its own, right on time, exactly as he had promised.

choices.label: Descend the opened stair.

nodes.body: Nadia lifted the heavy black receiver, and to her surprise, a dial tone hummed steadily in her ear. The phone still worked. She stood there a moment, receiver in hand, weighing the whole strange night against the comfort of a warm car and her own bed waiting somewhere beyond these locked doors. She had already solved half a mystery nobody else even knew existed, and that alone felt like enough of a story. All she had to do was dial and ask someone to come get her.

choices.label: Call home from the museum desk.

nodes.body: The stair brought Nadia down into a low stone chamber lit by nothing but the flashlight glow of her own phone, and she stopped in the doorway, breath caught in her throat. Shelves lined every wall, crowded with the kind of treasures the founder had meant the whole town to share one day, paintings and carvings and small glinting things, all of it collected quietly and never once shown to anyone. A sealed letter sat propped against one shelf, addressed to no one in particular. Beside it lay an old, careful treasure map. Nadia stood very still in the hush of the chamber, and understood that whatever she did next, she was the one who got to decide.

choices.label: Read the founder's sealed letter.
Study the old treasure map.
Decide who to tell.
Grab something to prove it.

nodes.body: Nadia broke the old wax seal carefully and unfolded the letter. In careful, sloping handwriting, the founder explained everything: he had hidden the collection to protect it, once, from people who would have sold it off piece by piece, and he had always meant to bring it back into the light before he ran out of time to do so. The very last line was not quite an ending. It pointed, gently, toward one final clue, a resting place for the whole truth, waiting somewhere close by for whoever had come this far to read it.

choices.label: Follow the letter's final clue.
Keep the letter safe and leave the rest.

nodes.body: Following the letter's last line, Nadia pressed against the shelf it described until a false panel gave way, swinging outward to reveal a thick, leather-bound catalogue tucked behind it. Inside, page after page, the founder had listed every single piece in his hidden collection, and beside each one, carefully noted, exactly where it truly belonged. It was not just a list of treasures anymore. It was the whole mystery, solved and organized and waiting, resting now in her own two hands under the chamber's dim light.

choices.label: Read the catalogue to the end.

nodes.body: Nadia sat on the cold floor and read the catalogue from its first page to its last, the founder's careful notes slowly untangling a whole history the town had half forgotten. Every rumor about him, every whispered accusation, fell away line by line, replaced by the truth of a quiet man who had only ever meant to keep his town's treasures safe until the right moment came. By the time gray dawn light crept through the rotunda far above her, Nadia understood it all: not only where the collection was, but why it had waited, and exactly how to finally give it back.

choices.label: Carry the whole truth up into the morning.

nodes.body: Nadia folded the letter carefully and slid it into her coat pocket, leaving every shelf exactly as she had found it. Something about the founder's quiet, careful words felt less like a treasure map and more like a trust, one she did not want to rush or hand over carelessly to strangers. She would honor it properly, in daylight, when there was time to explain things right. For now, the collection could keep waiting a little longer. It had already waited a hundred years, after all.

choices.label: Slip out with the letter and a plan.

nodes.body: The old treasure map, unrolled fully, marked a walled-up wing Nadia had never once seen on any tour, a whole hidden section of the museum sealed away behind plaster and time. She could follow it right now, while the trail was fresh and her nerve was still up. She could photograph it carefully instead and bring proof back to someone who could do this properly in daylight. Or she could simply sit here a while longer, tracing every faded line with her finger, memorizing a mystery that had waited this long already and would keep waiting a little more.

choices.label: Follow the map to the walled-up wing.
Photograph the map and go.
Keep tracing it as the sky greys.

nodes.body: Nadia followed the map's careful lines behind a section of plaster that gave way more easily than she expected, opening onto a small, sealed gallery. Paintings lined the walls, canvases the town had long assumed were lost forever, their colors dimmed but still unmistakably beautiful under her phone's light. She marked the way back with a careful line of chalk and photographed every frame she could reach, determined that whatever happened next, no one would ever be able to say this room had not existed.

choices.label: Bring the lost wing to light.

nodes.body: Nadia laid the old map flat and photographed it corner to corner, careful to catch every faded line and label clearly. Then she rolled it back up exactly as she had found it and set it down on the same shelf, leaving the whole chamber undisturbed behind her. Whatever happened next was not entirely her decision to make alone, she thought, but she would have proof enough, safe on her phone, to make sure the right people believed her when she finally told them everything.

choices.label: Head out with the proof.

nodes.body: With the letter and the catalogue both safe in her hands, Nadia sat with the bigger question a while longer: who should actually know about all this? The curator, she guessed, would do right by the founder's memory and make sure everything was handled properly. Her closest friends would keep the secret thrilling and private, a wonder shared only among themselves. Or she could simply leave everything exactly as it lay, trusting the museum to stumble onto its own history eventually, the way it probably should have years ago, without her help at all.

choices.label: Wake the curator with proof.
Keep it a secret with your friends.
Leave it for the museum to find.

nodes.body: Nadia gathered the letter and the catalogue carefully under one arm and waited by the staff door until the first gray light of morning crept across the rotunda floor. When the curator finally arrived, keys jingling, Nadia held everything out at once and simply started explaining. The curator's astonishment slowly gave way to something closer to wonder as she turned the catalogue's pages herself, realizing exactly what a school trip and one locked door had somehow managed to uncover overnight.

choices.label: Hand over the founder's trust.

nodes.body: Nadia decided, sitting alone in the quiet chamber, that this would be a secret kept carefully among her closest friends, at least for now. She pictured the three of them creeping back some future night, flashlights bobbing, sworn to protect a wonder that belonged, for the moment, only to them. It was not the whole truth given back to the town, not yet, but it was hers to share exactly as she chose, and that felt like its own small kind of treasure.

choices.label: Seal the secret with a promise.

nodes.body: Nadia carried the founder's own map back up to the guard's desk and propped it open where it could not be missed, a short note tucked beside it explaining just enough to point the way without giving away who had written it. She trusted the museum to follow the trail properly from there, with all its careful experts and proper tools, and finish what the founder himself had started so long ago. Some discoveries, she decided, were better handed over quietly than claimed.

choices.label: Leave the clue and slip away.

nodes.body: On the nearest shelf, close enough to touch, sat a small jewelled owl, its glass eyes catching what little light there was. One glance at it would prove her whole wild story in an instant, no explaining needed, no one doubting a single word. Nadia's hand hovered just above it, fingers not quite touching the cool metal. Taking it felt like proof. It also felt, somehow, like taking something that was not hers to take, not yet, not like this. She stood there weighing the two feelings against each other, unable to decide.

choices.label: Slip it in your bag and run.
Set it back where it belongs.

nodes.body: Nadia slipped the little owl into her bag and turned to go, already imagining the look on everyone's face when she showed them proof. She had barely reached the bottom step when a soft, unmistakable chime of bells rang out behind her, the shelf's weight having quietly reset the moment the owl left it. She kept climbing anyway, faster now, the gentle ringing trailing her the whole way up the stair and out into the hall, announcing exactly what she had done to anyone who cared to listen.

choices.label: Reach the hall with the bells ringing.

nodes.body: Nadia set the little owl back down onto its velvet exactly where it had rested, feeling the small weight of the decision settle more easily than she expected. The truth, she decided, was worth more than any single trophy, however proof it might have offered. She climbed back up the hidden stair with nothing in her bag but her own scribbled notes and a clear conscience, ready to explain everything honestly to whoever asked, without needing an owl to back her up.

choices.label: Leave with clean hands.

nodes.body: The guard's torch found Nadia standing right out in the open, and there was no pretending otherwise. He was not unkind about it, just firm, walking her briskly toward the lobby to wait for someone to come get her. The mystery, whatever it truly was, would have to wait for another night. Nadia sat on a hard bench under the bright lobby lights, cheeks warm with embarrassment, running through everything she had almost found. She was safe, and unharmed, and thoroughly caught, and there was nothing left to do now but wait quietly for morning.

The roped-off gallery's door clicked shut behind Nadia with a note of quiet finality, and no amount of pushing or pulling would open it again from the inside. She checked every edge of the frame twice before finally giving up. A low bench sat against the far wall, dusty but sturdy, and she settled onto it to wait out the rest of the night. It was warm enough, and perfectly safe, if a little dull. The founder's secret, whatever it truly was, would simply have to stay hidden a little longer, unbothered by her tonight.

Blind in the endless stacks, Nadia turned one too many corners and completely lost her sense of direction, unable to tell which way led back toward the lamps and which way led deeper into the dark. Rather than risk wandering further, she found a clear patch of floor and simply sat down to wait. It was uncomfortable, and dusty, and more than a little boring, but nothing about it was dangerous. Eventually, she knew, the museum's morning lights would click on somewhere overhead, and the locked cabinet would simply have to stay a mystery until then.

The glass gave way with a sharp, ugly snap, far louder than Nadia had expected, and the sound brought the night guard running within seconds. Nothing was hurt except the case itself, cracked clean across one corner, but that was more than enough. Nadia spent the rest of the night sitting in the lobby, explaining herself as best she could to one very unimpressed adult after another. The coded ledger stayed exactly where it had fallen, unread, its secrets safe for now behind a spray of broken glass she would rather forget.

High above the hall, the ladder finally ended at a hatch that had been bricked over so long ago the mortar had gone nearly black. There was nowhere left to climb. Nadia gripped the cold rungs and made her way carefully back down instead, legs shaking slightly by the time her sneakers touched solid floor again. She was tired, and a little dizzy from the height, but entirely unhurt. The rotunda, and whatever waited beyond it, would simply have to stay out of her reach for tonight.

Nadia rushed the timing, certain she had judged the swing correctly, and the great pendulum caught her anyway, its slow weight bumping her back firmly but gently onto the safe side of the doorway. No harm done, just a solid, undeniable nudge that left no room for arguing. She sat down right there on the cold floor for a moment, catching her breath. Whatever waited on the other side of the Clock Corridor, she decided, it had beaten her fair and square for the night.

Forced too hard and too fast, the brass rings locked solid, refusing to turn in either direction no matter how Nadia pulled or pushed. The hidden stair, wherever it led, stayed firmly shut beneath the pedestal. She sat down beside the silent globe, catching her breath and staring at the gears that would not budge for her tonight. It felt strange, being this close to whatever the founder had hidden and still completely unable to reach it, but there was nothing left to do except wait quietly for the museum to open in the morning.

The ringing bells led the guard straight to Nadia before she had even reached the lobby, his torch catching her mid-stride with the little owl still chiming softly in her bag. Red-faced, she handed it back and put everything right as best she could, explaining the whole strange night in a rush of words that somehow, mostly, made sense to him. He let her off with a stern word and a promise that she would come back properly, by daylight, to finish this the right way. The treasure stayed safe. The lesson, she suspected, would stay with her a lot longer.

Nadia lost herself completely in the old map's faded lines, tracing each faint mark until she nearly forgot where she was. When she finally looked up, the sky beyond the rotunda's glass dome had already gone gray with early morning, and somewhere below she could hear the first staff arriving for the day. She slipped out quietly through a side door before anyone noticed her, the mystery only half traced, half understood. She promised herself, walking home in the pale light, that she would come back and finish this properly, the right way, very soon.

The founder's forgotten workshop became Nadia's own quiet triumph. She photographed every unfinished automaton carefully, leaving each one exactly where it stood, disturbing nothing. By the time morning light crept through the rotunda's dome, she was waiting to tell the curator about an entire lost room nobody alive had known still existed, tucked behind a hidden panel for a hundred years. It was not gold or jewels she carried out with her, just proof of a marvel no one had dared hope had survived, and that felt like more than enough.

With the letter in one hand and the catalogue in the other, Nadia climbed the last stair and walked out into a morning brighter than any she remembered. She handed the founder's whole story back to the town that had half forgotten him, page by careful page, until his name was cleared and his hidden collection finally, properly found. The museum would never be quite the same again, and neither, she suspected, would she. A school trip that had left without her had somehow given her back an entire piece of history nobody else had known was missing.

Nadia left the chamber with nothing but the founder's letter folded carefully in her coat, and a quiet promise to herself about how she meant to honor it. Over the weeks that followed, she found small, careful ways to nudge the curator toward the truth, a dropped hint here, a gentle question there, never quite explaining how she knew. She had become, without ever asking for the role, keeper of a trust the founder had left behind, and she meant to carry it as gently as he once had.

The walled-up wing of lost paintings became Nadia's discovery to give, and give it she did. Her careful photographs brought restorers first, then reporters, and soon the whole town was crowding through doors that had stayed sealed for a hundred years, marveling at canvases nobody alive had ever expected to see again. Standing at the edge of the crowd, watching strangers gasp at what she had found in the dark, Nadia felt something warm and proud settle over her, quiet and entirely her own.

At first light, the curator listened to Nadia's whole story with her mouth slightly open, and then, without wasting another minute, gathered a careful team to follow her down the hidden stair. By midmorning, word had spread through the whole museum staff about the girl who had somehow given the founder's gift back to everyone, exactly as he had always wished it. Nadia stood a little apart from the fuss, tired and dusty and entirely content, having finally finished something that had waited a hundred years for her.

The hidden chamber became the treasured secret of Nadia and her closest friends, a wonder they visited quietly and guarded together, just the three of them. They never told another soul, not yet, and the not-telling became its own kind of bond, a promise stitched between them that felt every bit as valuable as anything on those dusty shelves. It was theirs alone for now, tucked safely under the museum's oldest floor, and Nadia found she did not mind waiting to share it properly.

Nadia left the founder's own map propped open on the guard's desk, a careful note tucked beside it, and slipped away into the gray morning before anyone arrived. Days later, the museum followed the trail exactly as she had hoped, uncovering the whole hidden collection at last. They never learned whose small, careful hands had set them on the path in the first place. Nadia kept that quietly to herself, content to know the founder's gift had finally reached the people he always meant to have it.

Nadia dialed home from the guard's desk and curled up in the warm lobby to wait for someone to come get her, the mystery still only half solved but safely written down in her own careful notes. It was not the full triumph she might have imagined, no crowd, no headline, no grand reveal. But it had been a real adventure, all the same, and she made herself a quiet promise to come back properly and finish what tonight had only started. For now, safe and a little proud, that felt like more than enough.

With the map safe on her phone and the hidden chamber left exactly as she had found it, Nadia slipped out into the gray dawn light without waking anyone. She had proof enough now to bring the right people back properly, in daylight, with all the care the discovery deserved. It was not her place, she decided, to make every decision alone in the dark. She would let the grown-ups take it from here, and simply feel proud of everything she had managed to find on her own.

Nadia left the treasures exactly where they belonged and climbed back up into the ordinary museum with nothing but her own scribbled notes for company. She had not solved everything, not quite, and some questions would probably stay unanswered for a while yet. But she had learned something about herself in that dark chamber, about what she was willing to do and what she was not, and she walked home as the sky lightened, tired, a little cold, and genuinely wiser than the girl who had walked in.
